ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pork organ porridge; mapped to the broader porridge food product class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for pork organ porridge; A0FKP (congee/rice porridge composite) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for pork organ porridge or equivalent offal congee dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pork organ porridge is one bowl of approximately 350 g, as commonly served at hawker centres in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Cháo Lòng (Pork Organ Porridge) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cháo Lòng (Pork Organ Porridge), cr_id G00010554, with confidence 0.95. Carbohydrate is reported as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB source; this is acknowledged as a likely data anomaly for a rice-based dish and is documented in notes. All other macros and micronutrients are taken as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Congee / Rice Porridge
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork organ porridge specifically. GI of 78 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice congee / plain rice porridge (GI range 78–90, mean ~78), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The pork organ components do not contribute meaningfully to glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100; because the HPB source records carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g (likely a data anomaly), the computed GL is 0.0 — this is a data artefact and the true GL for a 350 g serving of rice porridge would be substantially higher. The GI category is retained as 'high' based on the rice porridge base.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit pork organ porridge due to its extreme sodium content of 1050 mg/100g, driven by fish sauce, salt, and pork broth; if consumed, request reduced seasoning, use low-sodium broth, and limit portion to no more than 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI rice porridge base (GI ~78) causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit pork organ porridge due to its high-GI rice congee base (GI ~78), which causes rapid postprandial glucose spikes; portion size should be kept small (≤150 g), and the dish should be paired with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables to moderate glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g), elevated cholesterol (89 mg/100g), and high arachidonic acid load from pork organs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid frequent consumption of pork organ porridge due to its high sodium content (1050 mg/100g), cholesterol (89 mg/100g), and the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid burden from pork offal, all of which adversely affect blood pressure, lipid profiles, and vascular inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High arachidonic acid from pork organs amplifies inflammatory cytokine production; sodium worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit pork organ porridge as pork offal is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) and cholesterol that amplify inflammatory pathways, and the sodium content of 1050 mg/100g exacerbates joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Organ Porridge (Cháo Lòng; 猪杂粥) is a savoury Vietnamese-origin rice congee dish widely sold at hawker centres and food courts in Singapore. The dish consists of a slow-simmered rice porridge base cooked until the grains break down into a thick, silky consistency, served with a variety of pork offal including liver, intestine, kidney, stomach, and sometimes pork blood. The broth is seasoned with fish sauce, salt, and ginger, and the bowl is garnished with fried shallots, spring onion, and white pepper. The texture is soft and comforting, with a rich, savoury, slightly gamey flavour from the organ meats. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (19.1 g/100g) and fat (10.5 g/100g) from the pork organs, and carries a very high sodium load of 1050 mg/100g due to fish sauce and seasoned broth. Cholesterol is elevated at 89 mg/100g. The HPB source records carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g, which is a likely data anomaly given the rice base; the true carbohydrate content from the rice component would be meaningful. The glycemic index of the rice porridge base is approximately 78 (high), making this dish a concern for blood glucose management. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for patients with hypertension (extreme sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high-GI rice base), cardiovascular disease (high sodium, cholesterol, and pro-inflammatory organ fat), and rheumatoid arthritis (arachidonic acid burden). Pork organs are a good source of iron (1.14 mg/100g), B vitamins, and zinc, but these benefits are outweighed by the sodium and lipid concerns for most clinical populations.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat pork organ porridge?
Is pig organ porridge high in sodium?
What are the calories in pork offal congee?

How much protein is in pork organ porridge?
Is pig organ congee high in cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010554) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The carbohydrate anomaly (0.0 g/100g for a rice-based dish) is documented but does not reduce source confidence, as the HPB entry is the canonical approved source for this dish.
notes: CARBOHYDRATE ANOMALY: carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g by the HPB source for this rice-based dish. This is almost certainly a data entry error or a value reflecting only the organ meat component without the rice. The HPB value is preserved as-is per source fidelity rules, but users should be aware that the true carbohydrate content of a served bowl of pork organ porridge would be substantially higher due to the rice base. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): carbohydrate_g is 0.0 as reported by the HPB Singapore Foodo source (confirmed zero per HPB); this is not naturally zero — it is a known data anomaly for this rice-based dish and is flagged accordingly. sugar_g is 0.0 as confirmed zero per HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as confirmed zero per HPB source. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific pork organ porridge preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010554, and no supplementary data was available from USDA FoodData Central for an exact match. GLYCEMIC LOAD NOTE: GL per serving is computed as 0.0 due to the carbohydrate data anomaly (0.0 g/100g); the true GL for a 350 g serving would be approximately 27 based on an estimated 10 g available carbohydrate per 100 g from the rice component. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain rice congee.
